### Normalizing race time and sector time

#### Race time normalization

The first normalization step is about the final result. Before we can ask whether a pacing pattern was good or bad, we need a fair way to define what a good or bad race was for that runner. Raw finishing time is not enough. A 35-hour UTMB can be a very strong result for one runner and a disappointing result for another. It depends on pre-race ability, and it also depends on the race year, because weather, conditions, and small course differences can shift the whole field.

So I first build a baseline expectation for each runner's finishing time. The question is not: *how fast did this runner finish?* The more useful question is: **given this runner's UTMB Index, and given the edition they ran, how fast would we expect them to finish?** Once we have that expectation, the actual finishing time can be interpreted relative to it.

To estimate that baseline, I model race time with UTMB Index, race year, and their interaction:

$$
\text{Race time} \sim \text{UTMB Index} + \text{Race year} + \text{UTMB Index}:\text{Race year}
$$

The first term captures runner ability. Stronger runners, measured by UTMB Index, are expected to finish faster. The race-year term allows the whole edition to shift up or down. The interaction term allows the ability-time relationship itself to change by year. In other words, a 50-point difference in UTMB Index does not have to translate into exactly the same time difference in every edition of the race.

This interaction is not only a technical detail. In the fitted model, the UTMB Index effect is strongly negative, meaning higher-index runners are clearly faster on average. The interaction terms are also meaningful, especially in 2024 and 2025, where the p-values are well below 0.001. This suggests that the performance gradient by UTMB Index was not identical across editions. That is exactly why I do not want to normalize all years with one fixed line.

| Model term | Estimate | p-value | Interpretation |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| UTMB Index | -0.0487 h | <0.001 | Higher-index runners are expected to finish faster. |
| UTMB Index x 2023 | -0.0024 h | 0.042 | The index-time slope was slightly steeper than in 2022. |
| UTMB Index x 2024 | -0.0047 h | <0.001 | The index-time slope was clearly steeper than in 2022. |
| UTMB Index x 2025 | -0.0051 h | <0.001 | The index-time slope was clearly steeper than in 2022. |

The goal is not to build the final performance model of UTMB here. The goal is to create a practical baseline: **given this runner's ability and this race year, what would we expect their finishing time to be?**

Once we have an expected race time, we can calculate the residual:

$$
\text{Residual} = \text{Expected race time} - \text{Actual race time}
$$

Usually residuals are written as actual minus expected. I use the opposite direction on purpose, because it makes the interpretation more natural for this analysis: **higher is better**. A positive residual means the runner finished faster than expected. A negative residual means the runner finished slower than expected.

For example, if a runner was expected to finish in 36 hours but actually finished in 34 hours, the residual is +2 hours. They were two hours better than expected. If they finished in 38 hours, the residual is -2 hours. They were two hours worse than expected.

![](saved_files/race_time_normalization_diagnostics.png){ class="click-zoom" }

The diagnostic plot is mostly a sanity check. The left panel shows the expected race-time relationship with UTMB Index separately by year. The pattern is strong and in the expected direction: higher UTMB Index means lower expected race time. The lines are not identical across years, which supports the decision to include the year interaction.

The right panel shows the residuals around that baseline. They are not perfect. There is still a lot of spread, and the residual structure is not completely random. That is expected in a race like UTMB, where weather, execution, nutrition, technical skill, sleepiness, stomach problems, and simple bad days all matter. For this analysis, that imperfection is acceptable, because the model is not meant to explain every runner's result perfectly. It is meant to remove the dominant ability-and-year effect before studying pacing.

For the main analysis, I use a relative version of the residual:

$$
\text{Normalized race time} =
1 - \frac{\text{Actual race time}}{\text{Expected race time}}
$$

The relative version is more useful than the absolute residual because one hour does not mean the same thing for every runner. Being one hour faster than expected is a larger relative improvement for a 22-hour runner than for a 44-hour runner. The percentage version puts everyone on a more comparable scale:

- `0.05` means the runner finished about 5% faster than expected.
- `0` means the runner finished almost exactly as expected.
- `-0.05` means the runner finished about 5% slower than expected.

This becomes the outcome variable for the pacing analysis. Instead of asking only who was fastest overall, we can ask who performed better or worse than expected **relative to their own ability**.

**Comment for the real post:** this section should probably replace the current `Race time normalization` block in `projects/path_to_utmb/pacing_strategy_analysis.qmd`. The current post already explains why "relative to ability" matters in the introduction, so this version does not need to repeat that idea too heavily. The main story here is: raw finishing time is unfair, the baseline model creates an expected time, and the normalized race-time score becomes the outcome for the pacing analysis.



#### Sector time normalization

Race-time normalization tells us whether a runner finished better or worse than expected. But pacing is not only about the final result. It is about **how the runner distributed their race across the course**.

Raw sector times are not enough for this. A sector time depends on the runner's ability, the year, the sector length, the elevation profile, and the terrain. A fast runner will usually be faster in every sector, so comparing raw sector times would mostly repeat the same information as the final race time. What we need instead is a way to compare the **shape** of each runner's race.

For that, I convert each sector time into a **sector time share**:

$$
\text{Sector time share} =
\frac{\text{Sector moving time}}{\text{Runner's total moving time}}
$$

So if a runner spent 8% of their total moving time in one sector, that sector has a share of `0.08`. The important detail is that these shares sum to 1 for each runner. This means we are no longer asking only who was fast. We are asking how each runner distributed their total moving time across the course.

This is useful because two runners can have very different total finishing times but a similar pacing shape. And the opposite can also happen: two runners can finish in a similar total time, but one may spend more of their race early, while the other loses more time later.

The next step is to estimate the expected sector share. The question becomes: **given this runner's ability, the race year, and the sector, how much of their total moving time would we expect them to spend here?**

The model is:

$$
\text{Sector time share} \sim
\text{Race year}:\text{Sector}
+
\text{UTMB Index}:\text{Sector}
$$

The first part gives each sector its own baseline in each year. This matters because sectors are not equal units: some are long, some are short, some contain major climbs, and some are mostly descending or more runnable. The same sector can also change slightly by edition. The second part allows the relationship between ability and sector share to differ by sector. Stronger and slower runners do not necessarily distribute time across climbs, descents, and late-race sections in exactly the same way.

After predicting the expected sector shares, I normalize the predictions so that they again sum to 1 for each runner. This is important because sector shares are compositional: if a runner spends more share in one part of the course, they must spend less share somewhere else.

Then we calculate the sector pacing residual:

$$
\text{Sector pacing residual} =
\text{Expected sector share} - \text{Actual sector share}
$$

Again, I use the direction where **higher is better**. A positive residual means the runner spent less of their total moving time in that sector than expected. A negative residual means they spent more of their total moving time there than expected.

For example, if a runner was expected to spend 6% of their moving time in a sector but actually spent 5%, the residual is `+0.01`, or one percentage point better than expected. If they spent 7%, the residual is `-0.01`, or one percentage point worse than expected.

For the main pacing analysis, I also use the relative version:

$$
\text{Normalized sector pacing} =
1 - \frac{\text{Actual sector share}}{\text{Expected sector share}}
$$

This puts sectors with different expected sizes onto a more comparable scale. Being one percentage point faster than expected is not the same thing in a sector that normally takes 2% of the race and in a sector that normally takes 9% of the race. The relative version expresses the difference as a proportion of the expected sector share.

On this scale:

- `0.20` means the runner spent about 20% less share than expected in that sector.
- `0` means the runner spent almost exactly the expected share.
- `-0.20` means the runner spent about 20% more share than expected in that sector.

![](saved_files/sector_share_normalization_diagnostics.png){ class="click-zoom" }

The diagnostic plot shows that the model captures the main structure of sector shares. The left panel has visible bands because each sector has its own typical share of the race. That is expected: some sectors simply take a much larger part of the race than others. The predicted shares follow this structure, which means the model is learning the basic sector pattern rather than treating all sectors as interchangeable.

The right panel shows the residuals. They are not perfectly random. This is partly because sector shares are bounded and compositional: every runner's shares must sum to 1, so errors in one sector are connected to errors elsewhere. The pattern also suggests that the model is better as a practical baseline than as a perfect sector-by-sector explanation.

For this analysis, that limitation is acceptable, but it matters for interpretation. A positive sector pacing value does not mean the runner was objectively fast in that sector. It means the runner spent **less of their own race** in that sector than expected, given the sector, year, and ability baseline. This is exactly what we need for the pacing question: not raw speed, but how the race was distributed relative to expectation.

**Comment for the real post:** this section can probably be shorter in the final `.qmd`. The core story is: raw sector times are unfair, sector shares describe race shape, expected shares create a sector baseline, and normalized sector pacing tells us where a runner spent more or less of their race than expected.
